topic: Design Refresh Phase A EXP-A1 — typography tokens + Google Fonts + semantic palette foundation (HARD HOLD proposal review)
change_summary: First adversarial review of EXP-202 proposal. Focus per queue push: (1) Google Fonts vs zero-third-party; (2) --semantic- prefix vs --accent overwrite; (3) dark-theme token distinctness; (4) --ink-3 justification. Review does NOT unlock integration — HARD HOLD, operator group approval required.
our_argument_summary: EXP-202 is a DRAFT, FOUNDATIONAL proposal that adds three Google Fonts (Source Serif 4, Inter, JetBrains Mono) via <link> tags and introduces new CSS custom properties (--serif, --sans, --mono, --ink, --ink-2, --ink-3, --rule, and seven --semantic-* color tokens with -soft variants) across :root light, @media dark, and @media print blocks. No existing selector is changed; no consumer for the new tokens ships in this EXP. Its proposed cost envelope is zero visual change, ~60-90KB WOFF2 cold load, and a new third-party origin (fonts.googleapis.com / fonts.gstatic.com).

proposal_analytical_questions_addressed.verdict_flip_plausibility: N/A for this EXP — no WIN verdict shifts. The proposal is presentation-layer.
proposal_analytical_questions_addressed.new_tag_naming: The --semantic-* prefix is justified in the proposal's anticipated_objection #3 (avoid collision with existing --accent consumers until EXP-A5 migrates them). This is SOUND but collides on the dark-theme hex with the existing --heading token — see hole #2.
proposal_analytical_questions_addressed.alternatives_considered: Proposal surfaces three delivery alternatives (Google Fonts default, self-host, Bunny Fonts) and flags them for human group. Good. But the privacy framing is built on a false premise — see hole #1.
proposal_analytical_questions_addressed.unknowns_acknowledged: Proposal openly admits --ink-3 is speculative (notes_for_human_review_group bullet 3) and that bundling with EXP-A2 is a live trade-off. Honest about uncertainty.

focus_area_verdicts.reasoning: The proposal's objection #2 response asserts docs/index.html currently makes zero third-party requests. That is factually incorrect: line 7 of docs/index.html loads Google Analytics gtag.js from googletagmanager.com. The privacy tradeoff is 'add a second third-party origin' not 'introduce the first'. The operator group should weigh this choice against the existing gtag.js decision, not against a clean-slate posture. Recommend the analyst rewrite the privacy framing before routing to operator review.

focus_area_verdicts.verdict: SOUND IN PRINCIPLE, COLLISION IN PRACTICE
focus_area_verdicts.reasoning: The additive path (introduce --semantic-* tokens, migrate consumers in EXP-A5) is the safer sequencing and correctly avoids visual regression in A1. However, in dark theme, --semantic-accent (#7eb8da) is byte-identical to the existing --heading (#7eb8da). The EXP does not flag this and does not explain whether it is deliberate (semantic-accent IS intended to be the heading color in dark) or accidental. Either way, the silent collision complicates any future audit that wants to trace which selectors consume which token.

focus_area_verdicts.verdict: FAIL — WCAG 3:1 UI-component threshold not met for --rule in either theme
focus_area_verdicts.reasoning: Measured contrast: --rule dark (#2e2e4a) on --bg dark (#1a1a2e) = 1.30:1. --rule light (#e4ddcb) on --bg light (#ffffff) = 1.35:1. Both fall well below WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.11 (3:1 minimum for non-text UI components such as dividers/rules). Any consumer that paints borders or horizontal rules with var(--rule) will render near-invisibly for users with moderate low vision and in bright/mobile conditions. EXP-A8 is planned to run the full a11y audit, but tokens that ship with a known WCAG failure should not be landed in A1 on the theory that A8 will fix them — that inverts the integration order and risks producing visual regressions when intermediate EXPs (A2-A7) consume the broken token. The semantic-warn vs semantic-crit distinction also has residual color-blind risk (both are red/orange family, luminance delta modest) but text on --bg contrast for both is fine (6.99 / 4.93). The --semantic-accent/--heading collision (see focus 2) is a distinctness issue too.

Proposal openly admits --ink-3 is included because the external reviewer's mock uses three ink tiers, with no current consumer. A1 should not land unconsumed tokens on prediction; the cost of adding --ink-3 during EXP-A2 (once a prose-audit consumer is identified) is one line. Counter: pre-landing avoids an A2 token-churn increment. Net recommendation: drop --ink-3 from A1, or rename to --ink-faint-provisional so subsequent EXPs know its status. The contrast value (4.71:1 against --bg dark) only marginally clears AA body-text 4.5:1 — if A2 ends up using it for a very faint caption style, accessibility headroom is thin.

holes_found.description: Privacy objection response contains a false premise. The proposal states 'Current docs/index.html makes zero third-party requests' but docs/index.html already loads Google Analytics gtag.js from googletagmanager.com (line 7). This misstates the privacy status quo and biases the operator group review toward accepting Google Fonts under a purity frame that does not apply.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite objection #2 response. Acknowledge that gtag.js is already third-party. Frame the choice as 'add a second Google-owned origin' vs 'consolidate on self-hosted assets' vs 'accept both third parties but choose Bunny Fonts for the font axis'. If the group intends Phase A to tighten privacy, the honest move is to surface the gtag question alongside the fonts question in the same group review.

holes_found.description: --rule dark (#2e2e4a) on --bg dark (#1a1a2e) contrast is 1.30:1 — fails WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.11 (3:1 minimum for non-text UI components). --rule light (#e4ddcb) on --bg light (#ffffff) is 1.35:1 — same failure. Both themes ship a divider token that is near-invisible at AA. Any downstream EXP that applies var(--rule) for borders, horizontal rules, section dividers, or card edges will carry that failure forward. EXP-A8 is planned to audit a11y, but tokens should not land with a known WCAG regression already baked in.
holes_found.recommendation: Raise --rule dark to at least ~#3a3a5a (≈2.0:1, still weak) or #4a4a6a (≈3.1:1, meets AA) and --rule light to ~#c8c0a8 or darker. Add a comment in the :root block linking to the WCAG SC. Alternatively, defer --rule from A1 to the EXP that first consumes it, so the contrast target can be verified against the actual consumer.

holes_found.description: Silent hex collision: --semantic-accent dark (#7eb8da) is byte-identical to existing --heading dark (#7eb8da). The EXP does not flag, justify, or document this. If intentional, state so (semantic-accent IS the heading blue in dark); if unintentional, pick a different hex. As written, two named tokens point at the same value in one theme but diverge in the other (--semantic-accent light #1e5f8a vs --heading light #2E4057), producing per-theme aliasing that is hard to reason about later.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a one-sentence note in new_tokens_dark_override explicitly acknowledging the hex match and whether --heading is expected to migrate to --semantic-accent in a later EXP, or pick a distinct accent hex (e.g. #6aa8cd) so dark-theme selectors can disambiguate.

holes_found.description: Cold-load bandwidth estimate is undershot. Objection #1 response cites '~60-90 KB on cold load' for the three webfonts. Source Serif 4 variable (italic + 400/600/700, opsz axis), Inter multi-weight, and JetBrains Mono 2 weights together typically measure ~100-130 KB WOFF2 via Google Fonts' current subsetting. Under-reporting the cost weakens the objection-response and may mislead the operator group's bandwidth-vs-self-host decision.
holes_found.recommendation: Do a real cold-load measurement against the exact href proposed (not an estimate), or raise the cited range to 100-130 KB to be conservative. If the measurement comes in lower than current estimates, great — cite the measured number.

holes_found.description: Color-blind risk on --semantic-warn (#d99860) vs --semantic-crit (#d46a6a). Both sit in the red/orange family with modest luminance separation (ΔL* ≈ 6). Under deuteranopia/protanopia simulation they converge. EXP-A8 plans glyph+label pairing to mitigate, but if any intermediate EXP (A3 stance chrome, A4 chart legend, A5 palette cleanup) consumes --semantic-warn or --semantic-crit before A8 lands, those interim surfaces ship with the confusion.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) pick warn/crit hues with larger luminance separation at definition time (e.g. shift warn toward #e0b870 yellowier, crit toward #c04040 deeper red), or (b) sequence the integration so EXP-A8 glyph+label lands BEFORE the first consumer of these two tokens — which means reordering the suggested sequence, not just trusting A8 to clean up.

holes_found.description: Preconnect/stylesheet placement is under-specified relative to the existing Google Analytics gtag <script>. The exact_insert says 'immediately after viewport and before the first <style> block'. Google Analytics currently lives in exactly that region (lines 6-12). As written, the spec is ambiguous about whether the fonts <link> tags land before or after gtag. Placement matters: preconnect only helps if it lands early enough in <head> for the browser pre-scanner to pick it up; landing after an async gtag script is still fine but the EXP does not make that call explicit.
holes_found.recommendation: Reword change_1 to: 'Immediately after <meta name=viewport> and BEFORE the Google Analytics gtag <script> block'. Or acknowledge the gtag block and explicitly choose an ordering with a one-line rationale.

holes_found.description: --ink-3 lands without a consumer. Proposal acknowledges this in notes_for_human_review_group. The cost of adding --ink-3 later (in EXP-A2, once typography application identifies a faint-caption consumer) is a single line; the cost of landing and then removing it is two diffs plus churn on any intermediate proposal that picked up the token 'opportunistically'.
holes_found.recommendation: Drop --ink-3 from EXP-A1. Let EXP-A2 add it iff the prose audit produces a consumer. Alternatively, rename to --ink-faint-provisional so any consumer that grabs it is flagged as 'provisional — subject to A2 confirmation'.

holes_found.description: font-family:monospace literal counts cited in content_diff.change_3 are off. Proposal says '3 in sections.json, more in docs/index.html'. Actual: 6 in sections.json, 6 in docs/index.html. The number propagates from the HNOTE source (also cites 3), but EXP-A2's planned 'literal count is 0' test assertion starts from this baseline.
holes_found.recommendation: Update change_3 counts to the current actual values, and note that EXP-A2's diff surface will be correspondingly larger than a naive reader of this EXP would assume.

holes_found.description: Bunny Fonts framed as GDPR-friendly alternative but still third-party. Anticipated_objection #2 positions Bunny Fonts as a mitigation that keeps display=swap with 'zero self-host overhead'. True, but it does not equate to 'zero third-party requests' — Bunny Fonts serves from fonts.bunny.net, a separate origin with its own privacy policy (even if IP-scrubbed). The framing should clarify that only self-hosting zero-outs the third-party axis.
holes_found.recommendation: In objection #2, separate the two axes: (i) third-party request count (self-host = 0 extra, Bunny = 1 extra, Google = 1 extra), (ii) privacy posture of the host (self-host = local, Bunny = GDPR-proxy, Google = Alphabet). Make the operator's choice on each axis explicit.

holes_found.description: Build reproducibility criterion only guarantees byte-identical source HTML, not identical rendered output. Google Fonts can change the CSS served at the static href (add/remove unicode subsets, revise font-face declarations) without changing the href. integrity's build-reproducibility check cannot catch this; a visual regression will surface only in screenshot diffs.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a note to the acceptance criteria distinguishing 'source-byte reproducibility' (enforced) from 'rendered-pixel reproducibility' (not enforced, dependent on Google's CDN stability). Flag this as a self-host argument the group can weigh.

holes_found.description: CC4 'warm-grey ink-tokens on deep navy background' claim is asserted, not validated. --ink (#e8e6e1 warm off-white) on --bg (#1a1a2e deep cool navy) reads fine at body scale; --ink-2 and --ink-3 at caption/meta scale against cool navy can look dingy rather than elegant, because editorial convention typically pairs warm-on-warm or cool-on-cool. No prototype has been eyeballed, per the HNOTE's own 'prototype and eyeball' guidance.
holes_found.recommendation: Before operator-group approval, the analyst or operator should eyeball a live prototype (one page with --ink-2 body copy and --ink-3 caption against the current dark bg) and record the result in a screenshot attached to the EXP. If the warm/cool pairing looks muddy, shift ink tokens toward slightly-cooler greys (e.g., --ink-2 dark #b5b5b0) before committing.

stronger_arguments.description: The analyst could explicitly number the third-party origins before/after: before=1 (googletagmanager.com), after=3 (+ fonts.googleapis.com, + fonts.gstatic.com). A simple bullet list of origins in the proposal makes the privacy decision crisp.
sources: docs/index.html line 7 for gtag; EXP-202 change_1 exact_insert for fonts origins

stronger_arguments.description: The proposal could pre-compute and state the WCAG AA contrast for every new token × background pairing in a small table (dark, light, print). That would catch the --rule failure automatically and would future-proof any EXP-A8 a11y sweep against the tokens defined here. Small table, very high signal.

EXP could explicitly pin a Google Fonts CSS API date or commit a dated <link> variant (Google does not support this directly, but self-host solves it). If reproducibility is load-bearing for integrity, self-host is the only path that genuinely guarantees it.
sources: Google Fonts changelog behavior; integrity's build-reproducibility spec in CLAUDE.md

kernel_of_truth.description: The proposal is correctly structured as an additive, reversible, foundation-only EXP. It honors the HARD HOLD requirements (status, expansion_type, integration_blocked_until, human_note_source, companion_note all set correctly). It sequences tokens before consumers, which is the right order for a multi-EXP refresh. The anticipated-objections section is unusually thorough (five objections covered) and the counter-considerations cross-reference CC1-CC7 from the HNOTE. The kernel_of_truth field itself honestly concedes the external designer's point about one-font-does-every-job.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Structural correctness and honest framing do not rescue the token-level design choices. A foundation that ships with a WCAG failure in --rule, an undocumented hex collision between --semantic-accent and --heading, a privacy framing built on a factual error, and a speculative --ink-3 without a consumer is a foundation that subsequent EXPs will have to unwind or work around. Better to fix at A1 than to inherit the tech debt across A2-A8.

recommended_action_detail: This review does NOT unlock integration (per HARD HOLD — operator group approval is the gating step regardless of curmudgeon verdict). But the critical finding (privacy premise error) and the two major findings (--rule WCAG fail; --semantic-accent/--heading collision) should be addressed in a revision before the operator group is asked to approve. The moderate/minor findings can be tracked as annotations on the group-review packet. If the analyst chooses not to revise, surface the three blocker-severity findings explicitly in notes_for_human_review_group so the operator is not approving under a false privacy frame.
deeper_analysis: The proposal is a competent draft whose structure I have no issues with. The issues are at the level of the specific token values and the framing of one objection. The --rule contrast failure is a genuine defect — 1.30:1 in both themes is not debatable. The privacy premise error is also objectively checkable by opening docs/index.html and reading line 7. These two findings alone justify a revision pass before the operator group review. The --semantic-accent/--heading collision is more of a documentation gap than a defect — it might even be intentional — but the EXP should say so. Everything else is polish. The analyst's separation of A1 (foundation) from A2 (application) is the correct sequencing; the critique here is about the specific content of the foundation, not about the foundation-first approach. If the analyst revises and re-pushes, most of this review can fold into a short 'concurred, here are three fixes' acknowledgment. Worth noting: this review's verdict intentionally does not recommend approval or rejection — that is the operator group's call per the HARD HOLD. The curmudgeon's role here is to surface the foundation-layer defects, not to gate integration.

docs/index.html line 180 — @media print { :root { ... } } confirmed present with --bg/--text/etc already defined
